item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read the story several times as a translator, I have to say it’s a good freshman SCP. Although the end of the story is quite NOT an SCP style one and seems a little bit too simple, the plot touched me. Considering the author as a freshman, you GOT my +1, nice job!

item.title: Translation & an issue of the object class
item.content:encoded:
Just finished the Chinese version of this SCP and trying to reformat the webpage on SCP-CN!
The thing I'm wandering is that the object class at the beginning of the article is Object Class: Safe Euclid Keter isn't that be //Object Class: Safe Euclid Keter Neutralized" instead?

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Jomono8, instead of posting multiple comments in succession, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can make tabs or organise as notes, the neutralized entry needs to be in the front as for the olds procedures, you can use commands to organize.

item.title: Is a Joke
item.content:encoded: It's fits better as a J Scp, that asside is really good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• I really like the whole losing at X-Com vibe of the funding subplot here, but the bird plot doesn't support it well and vice versa.
  • The accidental discovery at a pet store is one of those SCPs-in-the-tall-grass things that's just implausible.
  • This is just structurally unpleasant and difficult to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love the first picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agree on the tabs slightly. Seems as though it would make more sense to have details on Project HOLOTHUROIDEA be on a different offset - although the Foundation would likely document that kind of a large-scale change to their operations it seems unusual for that documentation to be a summarized history of the Foundation attached to every single article of minor anomalies when it would be more efficient to link to a summary of the situation instead.

Otherwise good article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you are trying to do here.

But the ending ruins it all: the death of the bird feels disparate from the rest of the story where the Foundation has to make do with less funding (something which I myself also have problems with, though I guess they all end up as Headcanon issues, so I will not say)

- 1, unfortunately

item.title: Very good, would like to use in my own SCP
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this SCP. I have wanted to ask you if I could include it in my own first SCP? I have more details in the Wikidot PM that I sent you.

~Rogieon Council
AKA Four_r44

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For a first SCP, this is very good. Needs a bit of editting, but I'll give this an upvote as I like the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a lot of redundancy within this skip, however, it still turned out to be a good read. To resolve the repetitiveness, maybe do something like SCP-2317 and include multiple tabs for each revision.

I agree with Hope-Dystopia here, shortening down the revisions would make this a lot easier to read. The layers aren't very clear and overlap making it harder for some to understand. This is probably why you're getting so many down votes, don't quote me on that though.

The note from Site 92 Director Dr. Tsai at the end was actually kind of sad. I personally believe this is a good way to end the skip.

Good luck if you ever decide to start the canon for Protocol HOLOTHUROIDEA. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the writing is on point in terms of clinical tone, and the bird concept has the potential to be an SCP. However, I don't necessarily think the proposed canon has potential, and if it does, I don't think it should be included as the second half of a long SCP article - maybe you could write a canon/tale and backlink it to your article?

Because the way it's written now, it's not just your article that should have these heavily revised procedures, but every article. Trying to introduce a full overhaul of the state of the Foundation, relationships with major governments and GoIs, as well as all containment procedures on all articles is technically possible, but I just don't see people going along with it.

This SCP is a pet bird that opens locks. In terms of upending our understanding of the Foundation and all containment procedures, there's not much to draw people in or convince them to accept this new canon. If you really do want to push the canon, maybe shorten it down. A lot of it seems like redundant writing, and from a narrative point of view, reading very extensive containment procedures that ended up being fairly irrelevant to the nature of the SCP itself was a bit of a let down. There might be a better way to get this idea across, like in a single, shorter addendum rather than the current format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a good read. Thank you for it.

I just wonder where you got the funding thing from? It feels a tad out-of-scope for the entry. I get the story you're trying to tell, but if the Protocol HOLOTHUROIDEA and stuff about GOC being give weapons-free on The Foundation was something you invented for this bird's story, it's a bit much. That's a story that needs a canon series. I was reading that about GOC, and went "Wait. What!?" And you just gloss over it.

You can easily tell a story about The Foundation deeming the bird too unimportant and short-lived to worry about allocating funds to, without involving the Global Occult Coalition gunning down every SCP.

item.title: Amazing
item.content:encoded: I LOVED this SCP. I wish I could make my first SCP as good as yours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am so confused.

Is this a new canon? Or was Protocol HOLOTHUROIDEA a temporary side-effect of keeping 4106 contained?

The presence of Protocol HOLOTHUROIDEA in the article basically changes everything we know about the Foundation. Why was this necessary in order to tell the bird's story?

item.title: Okay
item.content:encoded:
So with this at least you got a ligit SCP, but at the same time the article is not complete.

1st issue is the format, this is how it works

Item#

Object Class:

Special Containment Procedures

Description

Etc, Addendum, Recovery, Test Logs, Interviews, footnotes, Etc

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Keter reclassification was made before the Foundation realized what happened at Site 92. Instead of death of 4106, they thought it was a containment breach (the Foundation was in a very poor state that communication between sites is barely possible) and since Dr. Tsai, the only person 4106 isn't hostile towards can't contain it, containing 4106 would be a lot more difficult.

Of course they can just duct tape 4106 in its cage, but containing unimportant anomalies was not something the Foundation had the freedom to do after Protocol HOLOTHUROIDEA. Hence the Keter: Containing it would take a lot of effort and the Foundation won't do it anyway.

It wasn't tagged as Keter because it's considered neutralized judging from the note at the end of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see why it was changed to Keter, its containment procedures seemed fairly easy to carry out. Also, why isn't it marked as Keter? Overall this scip was rather confusing to read.

item.title: Re: SCP-4106
item.content:encoded: Not bad for a first SCP in my opinion. Shaggy makes a few good points, but what really caught my attention was the utilization of pictures you have taken rather than edited. Not many articles do this and I think true photographs can turn out better than edited images. (Probably because it’s pretty easy to tell the difference, and a photo makes it seem more realistic.) I would very much enjoy seeing more articles with pictures taken by the author, especially because good ones show how much work has been put into it. Keep up the good work, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think the entire tangent on funding makes very much sense here. It seems far beyond the scope of the SCP's file, regardless if it was the first domino to have kicked things off.

I'll admit I'd be interested in seeing a story about that (decreased funding) as its own story divorced from a random anomaly, as we don't really see a whole lot of articles with a weaker Foundation, or one dependent on government funding.

I'd also say that the interjection of that narrative cripples the story of the bird; the attempt at pathos at the end came off as weak because of the distraction.

Not bad, but not particularly well-constructed.

item.title: SCP-4106
item.content:encoded:
This is my first SCP. Many thanks to RockTeethMothEyes for critiques.

My goal was to introduce an ordinary anomaly (a bird that opens locks) and show how the Foundation's decreasing funding affects the containment of Safe/Euclid anomalies. I hope you liked it!

I took the two bird photos.

Container house is from here: https://www.pexels.com/photo/glass-panel-white-frame-door-723876/
